fuzzy_prd:
We need a small utility that can convert date and time related values to and from a standard exchange format so different parts of our system can share them reliably. Right now every team handles this differently and it causes bugs when values are passed between services. The utility should handle all the common kinds of time-related data our business uses: plain calendar dates, times of day, combined date-and-time values, elapsed durations, calendar-offset periods, standalone years, year-and-month combinations, and recurring anniversary-style month-and-day values. For each kind, there should be two ways to represent the value: a compact numeric form for machine-to-machine efficiency, and a human-readable text form for logging and display. The numeric versus text choice should be driven by a simple flag on the request, and the flag should default to the compact form when not specified. The utility should gracefully handle bad input by returning a clean machine-readable error signal rather than dumping internal stack traces or implementation details — similar to how our payment validation module handles its own error responses. Errors should identify what went wrong using a stable code, not runtime internals. The interface should accept a direction flag (encoding vs. decoding), a type identifier, the value to process, and optionally the format preference. The whole thing should be runnable from a command line that reads a single request and writes a single result.

A couple small details to make sure we’re all aligned on. If the request JSON does not include the 'as_timestamp' field, it should default to true, so we use the numeric/compact wire form by default. We should not treat missing 'as_timestamp' as false.

On the text side, when we serialize with as_timestamp=false, the result should be a JSON-quoted string, like '"2013-08-21"'. That same quoted-string behavior also applies to period and month_day, since those only have one wire form anyway. The one exception here is year, which always emits a bare integer, so serialize output for year is never quoted. Also for LocalTime in text form, it should be a quoted ISO-8601 local time string and it should always be normalized to include seconds, even if the input didn’t have them, so if it only came in with hour and minute we append ':00' for the seconds field.

On the numeric side, durations with as_timestamp=true should serialize as a decimal number of seconds. If it’s a whole-second duration, we still always render exactly nine fractional digits, so PT1M becomes 60.000000000. That fractional part is carrying nanosecond-level precision. For LocalTime with as_timestamp=true, the output is a numeric array. It always includes hour and minute. Second only shows up if it actually carries information, meaning non-zero, and nanosecond is only included if it carries information, also non-zero. Example: 22:31:05.000829837 becomes [22,31,5,829837], and that last value is nanoseconds not microseconds.

One other leniency we do want: when deserializing a date, besides accepting the usual [year,month,day] array and ISO-8601 string forms, we should also accept a single integer meaning count of days since the epoch, where day 0 = 1970-01-01. That looser input is only for the 'date' type, not the others.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Period always uses a single wire form regardless of the as_timestamp flag: an ISO-8601 period string (e.g., P1Y6M15D). The as_timestamp flag has no effect on period serialization or deserialization.

MonthDay always uses a single wire form regardless of as_timestamp: an ISO-8601 month-day string beginning with a double dash (e.g., --01-17). The as_timestamp flag has no effect. Serialization emits the quoted string; deserialization outputs the canonical form without quotes.

Year is always represented as a bare integer on the wire regardless of as_timestamp. Deserialization accepts either an integer or a numeric string. The output is always the canonical year number (integer), not a string.

trigger_keywords: what error is returned for unsupported type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'type' field names a value that is not one of the 8 supported kinds (date, time, datetime, duration, period, year, year_month, month_day), the output is exactly two lines: 'error=unknown_type' followed by 'type=<the offending type name>'. Each line ends with a newline.

trigger_keywords: what error for bad op field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'op' field is neither 'serialize' nor 'deserialize', the output is exactly two lines: 'error=unknown_op' followed by 'op=<the offending operation name>'. Each line ends with a newline. The error does NOT include a 'type' line in this case.

trigger_keywords: what output when value cannot be parsed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a value cannot be parsed into the requested type, the output is exactly two lines: 'error=invalid_value' followed by 'type=<the requested type name>'. No stack traces or runtime exception messages are included.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how our payment validation module handles its own error responses
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error-handling behavior in Dispatcher.java's main/handleSerialize/handleDeserialize methods: errors are emitted as plain key=value lines (error=unknown_type, error=unknown_op, error=invalid_value) with no stack traces, and each error is accompanied by a contextual field (type= or op=) identifying the offending value.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question is not covered by the available constraint specifications. Please refer to the documented wire format contracts for each temporal type or ask a more specific question about a particular type, operation, error condition, or output format.

feature1_date.json.description: Round-trip a calendar date (year, month, day with no time-of-day and no zone) through the JSON codec. Serialization has two wire forms selected by a flag: a compact numeric array of the three date components, or an ISO-8601 extended date string. Deserialization accepts either of those wire forms and, leniently, a single integer interpreted as a count of days since the epoch; in every case the parsed value is reported in its canonical ISO-8601 date form so the conversion is observable.

feature2_time.json.description: Round-trip a wall-clock time-of-day (hour, minute, optional second and sub-second, no date and no zone) through the JSON codec. The numeric-array wire form lists the present components: hour and minute, plus second and nanosecond when they carry information. The string wire form is an ISO-8601 local time and is always normalized to include seconds even when the input omitted them. Deserialization accepts either wire form and reports the parsed value in canonical ISO-8601 time form.

feature3_datetime.json.description: Round-trip a combined date-and-time without zone offset through the JSON codec. The numeric-array wire form concatenates the date components (year, month, day) with the time components (hour, minute, and second/nanosecond when present). The string wire form is an ISO-8601 local date-time. Deserialization accepts either wire form and reports the parsed value in canonical ISO-8601 date-time form.

feature4_duration.json.description: Round-trip a time-based amount of elapsed time (a duration) through the JSON codec. The numeric wire form is a decimal count of seconds, where the fractional part carries sub-second precision down to nanoseconds and is always rendered with nine fractional digits when whole. The string wire form is the ISO-8601 duration notation. Deserialization accepts a number (interpreted as seconds) or the ISO-8601 string and reports the parsed value in canonical ISO-8601 duration form.

feature5_period.json.description: Round-trip a date-based amount of time expressed in years, months and days (a period) through the JSON codec. A period has a single wire form regardless of the timestamp flag: an ISO-8601 period string. Serialization emits that string; deserialization parses it back and reports the canonical ISO-8601 period form, in which components that are zero may be omitted.

feature6_year.json.description: Round-trip a standalone calendar year through the JSON codec. A year is represented on the wire as a bare integer. Serialization emits that integer; deserialization accepts either an integer or a numeric string and reports the parsed value as the canonical year number.

feature7_year_month.json.description: Round-trip a year-and-month value (a specific month of a specific year, with no day) through the JSON codec. The numeric-array wire form lists the year and the month number. The string wire form is an ISO-8601 year-month. Deserialization accepts either wire form and reports the parsed value in canonical ISO-8601 year-month form.

feature8_month_day.json.description: Round-trip a recurring month-and-day value (a calendar position with no year, e.g. an anniversary) through the JSON codec. A month-day has a single wire form regardless of the timestamp flag: an ISO-8601 month-day string beginning with a double dash. Serialization emits that string; deserialization parses it back and reports the canonical ISO-8601 month-day form.

feature9_errors.json.description: Report failures as a stable, language-neutral error contract instead of leaking runtime details. When a value cannot be parsed into the requested temporal type, the codec reports an invalid-value error naming the requested type. When the requested operation is neither serialization nor deserialization, it reports an unknown-operation error echoing the offending operation. Each error is emitted as plain key=value lines.
